--- SOURCE 2: Reshaping healthcare with wearable biosensors | Scientific Reports ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26951-z

SUMMARY:
This article focuses on wearable biosensors used for healthcare monitoring in different situations, as well as the development, technology, business, ethics, and future of wearable sensors for health monitoring. Graphene films were used in patches based on micropixel-based sensor arrays, and reverse ion electroosmosis was used to directly collect and measure glucose in the interstitial fluid of cells through hair follicles, allowing blood glucose levels to be monitored continuously and dynamically for a long time. The development of epidermal wearable biosensors has allowed them to be used to detect a variety of medications, such as a sweat-based wearable sensor that uses pilocarpine's ionic electroosmosis or exercise to create sweat from which to detect caffeine. A self-powered stretchy health monitor constructed of graphene material is one of the wearable sensors developed by researchers at Pennsylvania State University in the United States.

# Reshaping healthcare with wearable biosensors

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 13**, Article number: 4998 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

40k Accesses

7 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Wearable health sensors could monitor the wearer's health and surrounding environment in real-time. With the development of sensor and operating system hardware technology, the functions of wearable devices have been gradually enriched with more diversified forms and more accurate physiological indicators. These sensors are moving towards high precision, continuity, and comfort, making great contributions to improving personalized health care. At the same time, in the context of the rapid development of the Internet of Things, the ubiquitous regulatory capabilities have been released. Some sensor chips are equipped with data readout and signal conditioning circuits, and a wireless communication module for transmitting data to computer equipment. At the same time, for data analysis of wearable health sensors, most companies use artificial neural networks (ANN). In addition, artificial neural networks could help users effectively get relevant health feedback. Through the physiological response of the human body, various sensors worn could effectively transmit data to the control unit, which analyzes the data and provides feedback of the health value to the user through the computer. This is the working principle of wearable sensors for health. This article focuses on wearable biosensors used for healthcare monitoring in different situations, as well as the development, technology, business, ethics, and future of wearable sensors for health monitoring.

## Introduction

As the worldwide aged population has grown in recent years, the medical sector has been confronted with a new challenge: how to assist the patients in achieving health monitoring. Biosensors that enable real-time health monitoring, prevention, and treatment have been the emphasis in recent years. As smart wearable devices have become more popular, they can now be used to predict disease, and people improving their health beyond exercise through wearable devices will become a new trend[1](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR1 "Dunn, J. et al. Wearable sensors enable personalized predictions of clinical laboratory measurements. Nat. Med. 27, 1105–1112. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-021-01339-0
                  
                 (2021)."). Wearables are a burgeoning market, and their capabilities have evolved from tracking steps to monitoring medical issues and enhancing general health[2](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR2 "Ajami, S. & Teimouri, F. Features and application of wearable biosensors in medical care. J. Res. Med. Sci. 20, 1208–1215. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4103/1735-1995.172991
                  
                 (2015).").

Figure [1](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#Fig1) shows a conceptual demonstration of a remote monitoring system, from which wearable sensors are used to collect data on the wearer's physiological indicators to monitor the patient's own physical condition in real-time. The patient's data is passed on to the mobile device through wireless communication, and then the data is passed to the remote center via the Internet. When the system detects a patient's sudden condition, such as a fall, it sends an alert message to the emergency service center to provide timely assistance to the patient. In an emergency, the data-linked family members and caregivers are also alerted to each other to let the relevant personnel know the status of the current patient.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-26951-z/MediaObjects/41598_2022_26951_Fig1_HTML.png)

Schematic diagram of a remote health monitoring system based on wearable sensors[3](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR3 "Magno, M., Salvatore, G. A., Jokic, P. & Benini, L. Self-sustainable smart ring for long-term monitoring of blood oxygenation. IEEE Access 7, 115400–115408. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2928055
                  
                 (2019)."). Data on physical health is collected through the sensors worn and transmitted to healthcare workers through networked devices. Healthcare workers can use this data to take an action.

The major purpose of these sensors is to assist users in monitoring their own health and to provide timely warnings to customers who are ill. Wearable sensors have the main advantage of allowing consumers to conduct scientific health monitoring anywhere and at any time. Because the user visits the clinic for a diagnostic and the doctor utilizes the external sensor to make a diagnosis in a specific location, the user can save a lot of time if the portable sensor is worn. The advantages of wearable sensors for users are more prevalent, especially for users who use a range of portable sensors. For patients with chronic diseases, wearable devices can reduce the number of hospitalizations, because they can adjust their daily routines and diets in time according to the display of device data, which can save expensive treatment costs. Wearables can replace medical gear such as ECG gadgets and defibrillators by precisely measuring body temperature and heart health, which can help hospitals save money on equipment[3](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR3 "Magno, M., Salvatore, G. A., Jokic, P. & Benini, L. Self-sustainable smart ring for long-term monitoring of blood oxygenation. IEEE Access 7, 115400–115408. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2928055
                  
                 (2019)."). In addition, wearable devices can help patients better manage their lives and health, so that patients can understand their current physical condition in real-time through the device so that they can be hospitalized in a timely manner. People are paying more attention to their own health as their living standards improve, thus people’s attention to the heart, blood oxygen monitoring, sports and fitness, and related equipment has expanded dramatically[4](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR4 "Soh, P. J., Vandenbosch, G. A. E., Mercuri, M. & Schreurs, D. M. M. P. Wearable wireless health monitoring: Current developments, challenges, and future trends. IEEE Microw. Mag. 16, 55–70. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/MMM.2015.2394021
                  
                 (2015).").

Sensor detection for a variety of chronic and acute disorders has been studied since the nineteenth century. The pacemaker is the first sensor for monitoring human health by sending electrical impulses to the heart to allow it to beat more consistently[5](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR5 "Yoon, S., Sim, J. K. & Cho, Y.-H. A flexible and wearable human stress monitoring patch. Sci. Rep. 6, 23468. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/srep23468
                  
                 (2016)."). Humans began to utilize wearable sensors for health purposes as early as 1960[6](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR6 "Jeffrey, K. & Parsonnet, V. Cardiac pacing, 1960–1985. Circulation 97, 1978–1991. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1161/01.CIR.97.19.1978
                  
                 (1998)."). According to research by Jeffrey and Parsonnet, the first pacemaker was implanted in a 40-year-old patient in 1958 by thoracic surgeon Erik Senning of Karolinska Hospital in Stockholm, who also implanted a pulse generator with rechargeable NiCd batteries[6](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR6 "Jeffrey, K. & Parsonnet, V. Cardiac pacing, 1960–1985. Circulation 97, 1978–1991. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1161/01.CIR.97.19.1978
                  
                 (1998)."). The pacemaker's progress much like other devices has been amazing and swift.

People are becoming more enthusiastic about the future of health-related sensors. Different wearable health sensors[7](#ref-CR7 "Pei, X. et al. A bifunctional fully integrated wearable tracker for epidermal sweat and wound exudate multiple biomarkers monitoring. Small 18, 2205061 (2022)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Sempionatto, J. R., Lasalde-Ramírez, J. A., Mahato, K., Wang, J. & Gao, W. Wearable chemical sensors for biomarker discovery in the omics era. Nat. Rev. Chem. 6, 899–915 (2022)."),[9](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR9 "Wang, M. et al. A wearable electrochemical biosensor for the monitoring of metabolites and nutrients. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 6, 1225–1235 (2022).") and implantable sensors[10](#ref-CR10 "Calero, D., Paul, S., Gesing, A., Alves, F. & Cordioli, J. A. A technical review and evaluation of implantable sensors for hearing devices. Biomed. Eng. Online 17, 1–26 (2018)."),[11](#ref-CR11 "Wu, T., Redouté, J.-M. & Yuce, M. R. A wireless implantable sensor design with subcutaneous energy harvesting for long-term IoT healthcare applications. IEEE Access 6, 35801–35808 (2018)."),[12](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR12 "Wang, L., Jiang, K. & Shen, G. Wearable, implantable, and interventional medical devices based on smart electronic skins. Adv. Mater. Technol. 6, 2100107 (2021).") as well have been created and utilized since then. When the sensor is functional, it is important to know how to acquire the patient's health information and new technology with phone apps are helping make that possible.

In recent years, the face of wearables has shifted dramatically, with researchers shifting their attention from tracking people's regular exercise routines to addressing important difficulties in healthcare applications such as diabetic patient management and geriatric remote monitoring. To meet these big obstacles, scientists have focused their efforts on developing wearable biosensors, which are defined as sensing devices that incorporate biometric aspects into sensor operations, such as enzymes, antibodies, cell receptors, or organelles.

The sensor unit, first and foremost, is a required feature of all modern smart gadgets and comprises of many circuit boards that assemble sensors. The data collected by the sensors is sent to a central processing module, such as a personal digital assistant (PDA). The acceleration sensor is mounted on a pair of pants, and the circuit board is connected to a laptop in Laerhoven and Cakmakci's experiment. The circuit board will send data from the acceleration sensor on the user's pants to the laptop when the user is exercising[13](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR13 "Van Laerhoven, K. & Cakmakci, O. What shall we teach our pants? Fourth International Symposium on Wearable Computers (ISWC 2000) (2000)."). For a long time, Lee and Mase have used sensor units to detect user behavior; they use a gyroscope, an angular velocity sensor, and an acceleration sensor to detect user behavior; these sensors are connected to a Linux PDA[14](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR14 "Seon-Woo, L. & Mase, K. Activity and location recognition using wearable sensors. IEEE Pervasive Comput. 1, 24–32. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/MPRV.2002.1037719
                  
                 (2002)."), which is a sensor unit; the hardware for data processing is smaller than that of a notebook, and meets the standard of portability of the monitoring system and does not interfere with normal life which places a crucial role in sensor device design.

Sensors can be physically attached to the body, via avenues of standard clothing, watches, mobile phones, and other items that can be embedded with sensors in daily life. Flexible sensors are these types of sensors, and today's advances in printed electronics and materials allow some tiny sensors to be worn as skin patches[15](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR15 "Valenza, G. et al. Wearable monitoring for mood recognition in bipolar disorder based on history-dependent long-term heart rate variability analysis. IEEE J. Biomed. Health Inform. 18, 1625–1635. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/JBHI.2013.2290382
                  
                 (2014)."). These biosensors can measure skin conductance, heart rate, and body temperature, as well as detect changes in pH, glucose, and salt in the human body[16](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR16 "Pang, C. et al. Highly skin-conformal microhairy sensor for pulse signal amplification. Adv. Mater. 27, 634–640. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201403807
                  
                 (2015)."),[17](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR17 "Vega, K. et al. in Proceedings of the 2017 ACM International Symposium on Wearable Computers 138–145 (Association for Computing Machinery, 2017).") (Table [1](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#Tab1)).

Smart gadgets can be used to track data with greater accuracy, thanks to advancements in miniaturization technology. They can be woven into clothing as in-textile electronics, implanted into our everyday necessities, or even buried in the human ear. One of the most promising areas for wearable healthcare technologies right now is remote patient monitoring[18](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR18 "Li, Y., Zheng, L. & Wang, X. Flexible and wearable healthcare sensors for visual reality health-monitoring. Virtual Real. Intell. Hardw. 1, 411–427. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vrih.2019.08.001
                  
                 (2019)."). Wearable smart goods have also evolved from counting steps to monitoring physical health, providing healthcare staff with vital information such as blood pressure readings and potential arrhythmias.

Regarding smartphones, their role has expanded to include not only monitoring health but also carrying out simple, right, and healthy life interventions for users[19](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR19 "Stephens, J. & Allen, J. Mobile phone interventions to increase physical activity and reduce weight: A systematic review. J. Cardiovasc. Nurs. 28, 320–329. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1097/JCN.0b013e318250a3e7
                  
                 (2013)."),[20](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR20 "Wang, J. et al. Smartphone interventions for long-term health management of chronic diseases: An integrative review. Telemed. e-Health 20, 570–583. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1089/tmj.2013.0243
                  
                 (2014)."). Some programs can also provide simple remedies[21](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR21 "Gravenhorst, F. et al. Mobile phones as medical devices in mental disorder treatment: An overview. Pers. Ubiquit. Comput. 19, 335–353. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00779-014-0829-5
                  
                 (2015)."), such as mental diseases, because of the high frequency of daily use of smartphones and the high degree of user dependence. According to market research firm Counterpoint, the smartwatch market's future development focus is still fitness and health applications, therefore blood oxygen monitoring functions are common in existing wearable devices[3](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR3 "Magno, M., Salvatore, G. A., Jokic, P. & Benini, L. Self-sustainable smart ring for long-term monitoring of blood oxygenation. IEEE Access 7, 115400–115408. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2928055
                  
                 (2019)."),[22](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR22 "Scheffler, M. & Hirt, E. Wearable devices for telemedicine applications. J. Telemed. Telecare 11, 11–14. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1258/1357633054461994
                  
                 (2005)."). On another note, breast cancer has always been the leading cause of cancer death in women, and Cyrcadia Health, an American medical biosensor company, has been working with Jabil Medical Technology to develop a bra that can detect breast cancer, hoping to provide users with an early diagnosis of breast cancer. The application of this healthcare technology also marks another trend of change in wearable devices, from wrist-worn devices to body-wearable devices. Smart clothing built for specific conditions has been shown to be more practical, comfortable, durable, and reliable than smartwatches or bracelets[23](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR23 "Forbes, C. Wearable Healthcare Technology: Huge Potential. 
                  https://www.jabil.com/blog/healthcare-wearables.html
                  
                ."). Smart clothing built for specific conditions has been shown to be more practical than smartwatches and bracelets as certain demographics of the population, including children, the elderly, and individuals with chronic illnesses as well as mental health disorders, may find electronically embedded clothing to be especially helpful. Clothing could be designed ergonomically to fit the wearer’s body shape; the functionality could be maximized without negatively intrude the user’s personal space[24](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR24 "Biosolution, T. Smart Apparel vs. Wristband Based Trackers: A Study (2018).").

Figure [2](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#Fig2) shows the thought process of developing wearable devices and their sensor designs. The figure illustrates seven potential types of wearable devices ranging from smart garments to implants and these devices are all dependent on the sensor device design. Every psychological signal has a different sensor that can detect and measure the signal; therefore, the sensor design application will dictate the wearable form that the device will become. Not only do the sensors matter but the materials used for the biosensors are also taken into consideration like biocompatible wearable sensors or even self-healing flexible wearable sensors. Currently, more biosensors are becoming miniaturized and wireless as technology is advancing. This will be discussed later in the report[25](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR25 "Sharma, A., Badea, M., Tiwari, S. & Marty, J. L. Wearable biosensors: An alternative and practical approach in healthcare and disease monitoring. Molecules 26, 748 (2021).").

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-26951-z/MediaObjects/41598_2022_26951_Fig2_HTML.png)

Application principles and categorical examples of wearable biosensors[25](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR25 "Sharma, A., Badea, M., Tiwari, S. & Marty, J. L. Wearable biosensors: An alternative and practical approach in healthcare and disease monitoring. Molecules 26, 748 (2021).").

## Different sensor designs

### Wearable sweat biosensor

Because skin covers so much of our body, skin-worn devices get a lot of attention in many types of wearable biosensors. Real-time analysis of biomarkers in biological fluids and real-time monitoring in several biomedical and fitness applications are possible using epidermal biosensors. Optical, electrochemical, and mechanical are common transduction modes used in skin-worn biosensors that bind to biocatalytic and ion recognition receptors[33](#ref-CR33 "Mishra, R. K. et al. Detection of vapor-phase organophosphate threats using wearable conformable integrated epidermal and textile wireless biosensor systems. Biosens. Bioelectron. 101, 227–234. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2017.10.044
                  
                 (2018)."),[34](#ref-CR34 "Sempionatto, J. R. et al. Epidermal enzymatic biosensors for sweat vitamin C: Toward personalized nutrition. ACS Sensors 5, 1804–1813. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.0c00604
                  
                 (2020)."),[35](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR35 "Imani, S. et al. A wearable chemical–electrophysiological hybrid biosensing system for real-time health and fitness monitoring. Nat. Commun. 7, 11650. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms11650
                  
                 (2016)."). Most of the reports in recent years have focused on electrochemical and colorimetric transduction methods that have led to significant advances in skin-wearable devices, allowing for easy sampling of epidermal bio-stream so that the wearer does not feel uncomfortable, such as the use of electronic skin or printed temporary tattoos. In addition, miniaturization of sensors into wristbands or embedding sensors into clothing ensures close contact with the skin while allowing the sensor to withstand the forces generated during body movement. Sweat glands are distributed throughout our bodies and are the most readily available biological fluid in chemical sensing applications. Sweat production can be achieved by exercise, or naturally in an environment with a relatively high body temperature, and then by pressure or electroosmotic stimulation[36](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR36 "Baker, L. B. Physiology of sweat gland function: The roles of sweating and sweat composition in human health. Temperature 6, 211–259. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/23328940.2019.1632145
                  
                 (2019)."). In general, sweat contains various metabolites (such as lactate and urea) and minerals, and these biosensors perform a large amount of original humoral analysis for noninvasive monitoring of physiological health status and disease diagnosis management[37](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR37 "Hair, M. E., Mathis, A. I., Brunelle, E. K., Halámková, L. & Halámek, J. Metabolite biometrics for the differentiation of individuals. Anal. Chem. 90, 5322–5328. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.8b00414
                  
                 (2018)."). The epidermis is monitored noninvasively, which removes the issues associated with blood collection while still preserving the integrity of the protective stratum corneal skin. More research trials are needed, however, to show sweat's clinical utility as a diagnostic biofluid[36](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR36 "Baker, L. B. Physiology of sweat gland function: The roles of sweating and sweat composition in human health. Temperature 6, 211–259. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/23328940.2019.1632145
                  
                 (2019)."). Because sweat analytes are mostly delivered into sweat via capillaries, establishing a clear and accurate link with blood concentrations is difficult. Here it is possible to monitor the rate change of sweat by simultaneously monitoring an analyte or skin impedance measurement of concentration distribution independent of sweat rate[35](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR35 "Imani, S. et al. A wearable chemical–electrophysiological hybrid biosensing system for real-time health and fitness monitoring. Nat. Commun. 7, 11650. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms11650
                  
                 (2016)."),[38](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR38 "Brothers, M. C. et al. Achievements and challenges for real-time sensing of analytes in sweat within wearable platforms. Acc. Chem. Res. 52, 297–306. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.8b00555
                  
                 (2019)."). But there is also a problem here, the degree of dilution of the analyte during the excretion of sweat is affected by the rate of sweat and the rate of analyte distribution. Also, the epidermal biosensor has a target for measuring the concentration of analytes in the interstitial fluid (ISF). Within the active skin tissue of an organism, skin cells are surrounded by ISF, which provides nutrients, so there is a reliable correlation between blood and the concentration of ISF in many analytes. To evaluate ISF analytes in a non-invasive manner, these components must be extracted onto the surface of the skin, which can be done by reverse ion electroosmosis or ultrasonic electroosmosis. However, these methods are still flawed, and their accuracy will be affected by changes in extraction efficiency and skin surface contamination. Solving these problems requires advanced sampling methods and improvements to analyte monitoring methods[38](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR38 "Brothers, M. C. et al. Achievements and challenges for real-time sensing of analytes in sweat within wearable platforms. Acc. Chem. Res. 52, 297–306. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.8b00555
                  
                 (2019).").

The epidermal wearable biosensor was first designed for detecting a single analyte for a variety of target analytes. This proof of concept is carried out using new compression-resistant materials and sensor structures to achieve a high degree of skin fit. This is critical for reliable and stable sweat sampling during exercise, such as electronic skin or temporary tattoos, in combination with screen-printed flexible electrodes, providing an appealing platform for skin-based biosensors[39](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR39 "Ghaffari, R. et al. State of sweat: Emerging wearable systems for real-time, noninvasive sweat sensing and analytics. ACS Sensors 6, 2787–2801. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.1c01133
                  
                 (2021)."),[40](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR40 "Kim, J., Campbell, A. S., de Ávila, B.E.-F. & Wang, J. Wearable biosensors for healthcare monitoring. Nat. Biotechnol. 37, 389–406. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-019-0045-y
                  
                 (2019)."). As the skin has appealing electrochemical characteristics, it is ideal to design the electronic skin or temporary tattoos being in direct, continuous touch, and consistent flexibility with the base skin. Tattoo-based epidermal biosensors have now been shown to monitor critical sweat electrolytes and metabolites in real-time and non-invasively[41](#ref-CR41 "Bandodkar, A. J. et al. Tattoo-based noninvasive glucose monitoring: A proof-of-concept study. Anal. Chem. 87, 394–398. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/ac504300n
                  
                 (2015)."),[42](#ref-CR42 "Kim, J. et al. Noninvasive alcohol monitoring using a wearable tattoo-based iontophoretic-biosensing system. ACS Sensors 1, 1011–1019. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.6b00356
                  
                 (2016)."),[43](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR43 "Windmiller, J. R. & Wang, J. Wearable electrochemical sensors and biosensors: A review. Electroanalysis 25, 29–46. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/elan.201200349
                  
                 (2013)."). The first demonstrated continuous monitoring of lactic acid levels in sweat via an epidermal electrochemical biosensor, provides a dynamic real-time graph of an organism's lactate sweat during exercise[44](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR44 "Jia, W. et al. Electrochemical tattoo biosensors for real-time noninvasive lactate monitoring in human perspiration. Anal. Chem. 85, 6553–6560. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/ac401573r
                  
                 (2013)."),[45](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR45 "Yu, M. et al. Gold nanostructure-programmed flexible electrochemical biosensor for detection of glucose and lactate in sweat. J. Electroanal. Chem. 882, 115029. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2021.115029
                  
                 (2021)."). Sweat lactic acid is a by-product of the metabolism of local sweat glands in the skin. When the organism undergoes vigorous exercise, it leads to a higher production rate of sweat lactate. Although lactic acid does not respond immediately to contemporary blood levels, it does indicate the amount of physical exertion consumed during long-term exercise and can be utilized as a metric of exercise efficiency without invasive blood sampling. The subject of study was requested to wear an electronically printed temporary tattoo biosensor in the study. Lactate oxidase is added to the sensor to measure the sweat lactate generated during exercise. The measurements confirmed that sweat lactate did indeed increase with the intensity of exercise[44](/articles/s41598-022-26951-z#ref-CR44 "Jia, W. et al. Electrochemical tattoo biosensors for real-time noninvasive lactate monitoring in human perspiration. Anal. Chem.

ABSTRACT
--------

Wearable biosensor systems represent a significant breakthrough in the life sciences, offering real-time monitoring and quantitative assessment of various human health parameters. The escalating demand for continuous and immediate surveillance of both acute and chronic conditions, compounded by constraints in clinical infrastructure, has propelled extensive investigation into wearable biosensors. These systems have emerged as pivotal tools for furnishing physiological insights and facilitating non-invasive monitoring. They monitor various analytes through the analysis of sweat, epidermal, Interstitial fluids, breath and tears, and easily incorporated into flexible substrates ensuring long-term wear and continuous monitoring. However, these biosensors penetrate deeper into the body's fluid networks, providing a systemic approach to continuous monitoring of biomarkers, which is particularly useful for individuals with metabolic disorders. This comprehensive review explores recent trends and diverse applications of wearable biosensors. These applications empower individuals to dynamically assess their well-being, enabling informed healthcare decisions tailored to individual needs. The convergence of these approaches in wearable biosensors highlights a comprehensive approach to understand individual health. This integration holds the promise of transforming healthcare practice by providing continuous observance and remote monitoring. The result has been a paradigm shift towards active, personalized health care, leading to improved health outcomes and improved quality of life

1. Introduction
---------------

The increasing focus on biosensor advancement is attracting significant interest in biomedicine and healthcare sectors, primarily because of their extensive application in clinical practice, pharmacy, and routine healthcare monitoring [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0001),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0002)]. A biosensor is a combined receptor-transducer analytical device designed to detect alterations in biological processes and convert them into electrical signals[2]. They encompass a biological sensing component (enzymes, nucleic acids, proteins, etc.) and a physicochemical transducer that converts chemical reactions into discernible electrical signals correlating with the concentration of the analyte [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0003)] as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#fig0001).

The advantages of biosensors include their cost-effectiveness, compact size, swift and straightforward operation, as well as superior selectivity and sensitivity. One notable example is the glucose oxidase biosensor utilized by individuals with diabetes to monitor blood sugar levels [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0004)]. However, the routine monitoring of blood sugar levels depends significantly on in-person appointments, manual examinations, and treatment methods that involve substantial human interaction [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0005),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0006)]. Also, these biosensors are often crafted from rigid materials, and rely on invasive blood extraction at specific intervals limiting their comfort and suitability for extended wear [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0007)]. In contrast, the emergence of wearable biosensors enables the detection and monitoring of biomarkers and therapeutic substances from bodily fluids (tears, sweat, interstitial fluid, and saliva) in a minimally invasive or noninvasive and continuous manner [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0008)]. They are compact electronic gadgets that incorporate sensors either onto or within the human body, taking forms such as eyeglasses, smart watches, tattoos, gloves, clothing, or implants facilitating in vivo sensing, data recording, and computation using mobile or portable devices [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0010)]. They offer vital insights into the wearer's current condition and are poised to become a compelling option due to their notable attributes such as affordability, portability, sensitivity, timely diagnosis, and management of medical conditions, especially for managing chronic diseases [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0011)]. Wearable technologies have undergone progressive development, combining multiplexed biosensing, microfluidic sampling, and transport systems with flexible materials and body attachments to enhance wearability and user convenience. In the evolution of wearable biosensors, the incorporation of sensors to detect a wide range of biomarkers poses a significant challenge, necessitating continuous advancements in sensing technology. Electrochemical-based sensors stand out among various types of biosensors due to their advantages in ease of construction, heightened sensitivity, rapid response, and low power consumption. The constitution of wearable sensors heavily relies on sensing electrodes, predominantly employing the electrochemical approach. However, challenges persist regarding critical aspects like functional materials and manufacturing techniques employed in the fabrication of sensing electrodes, underscoring the need for improvement to enhance the performance of wearable biochemical sensors [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0012)]. Currently, Internet of Things (IoT) is a highly intriguing subject that offers solutions to overcome these constraints in multiple ways. Certain features, notably the inclusion of wireless power and data transmission capabilities, have markedly heightened the demand for wearable biosensors in the context of remote monitoring and Internet of Things (IoT)-driven sensing applications. Wearable devices, as part of the Internet of Things (IoT), have been developed to assist individuals in receiving appropriate medical care. These smart devices are connected to the internet to collect and transmit data, as well as receive information to facilitate informed decisions, evolving from simple accessories to more specialized and practical utilities. Additionally, these smart wearables can interface with other device groups, such as smartphones, for communication and computational purposes [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0015)]. Furthermore, these platforms exhibit outstanding attributes, including exceptional flexibility and stretchability, coupled with remarkable sensitivity, enabling them to discern a broad spectrum of signals with precision. However, further studies, including cohort validation and performance evaluation, are necessary to establish the clinical reliability and acceptance of wearable biosensors. In recent times, considerable attention has been directed towards developing wearable sensors, reflecting a growing focus on identifying diverse biomarkers that influence health. With advancements in miniaturized devices and technologies such as microelectronics and wireless communication, wearable biochemical sensors have become deeply integrated into our daily lives. Nevertheless, further development is necessary for the future [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0017)].

This comprehensive review critically explores the adaptation of wearable sensors for detecting a variety of analytes, including body fluids. The paper highlights the application of wearable biosensors in clinical practice and healthcare monitoring. It delves into previously conducted research, elucidating the underlying working principles, fabrication methodologies, and the utilization of flexible electrochemical biosensors incorporating nanoparticles and bio receptors for precise analyte detection. It explores the principles underlying biosensors, highlighting their role in detecting and converting biological processes into electrical signals. The paper discusses the advantages of biosensors, such as cost-effectiveness and superior sensitivity, as well as their limitations, including invasiveness and rigidity. A key novelty of this review lies in its systematic classification of wearable biosensing technologies based on the specific biological secretions, namely tears, sweat, epidermal fluids, interstitial fluid (ISF), and exhaled breath. By adopting this secretion-specific framework, the review provides a unique perspective on the design considerations, analytical performance, and application-specific challenges associated with each fluid type. Moreover, it highlights the emergence of next-generation wearable biosensors capable of enabling continuous, non-invasive monitoring, thereby paving the way for decentralized and proactive healthcare models. The review concludes by outlining future research directions, advocating for the development of biocompatible materials, miniaturized power sources, and multi-analyte detection platforms. By addressing both current capabilities and unmet needs, this work provides a valuable roadmap for advancing wearable biosensor technology toward robust, market-ready solutions.

2. Wearable Biosensors: a holistic approach to monitoring health
----------------------------------------------------------------

With advancements in sensor and operating system hardware technology, wearable devices have seen a gradual expansion of functionality and an improvement in the accuracy of physiological indicators. These sensors are now characterized by their increased precision, continuous monitoring capabilities, and enhanced comfort, making significant contributions to personalized healthcare [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Their primary purpose is to assist users in monitoring their own health and providing timely alerts to those in need. Wearable sensors offer the distinct advantage of enabling users to engage in scientific health monitoring at any time and in any location. This eliminates the need for frequent clinic visits and allows users to save valuable time by wearing portable sensors.

The benefits of wearable sensors are particularly notable for users who make use of a variety of portable sensors. For individuals with chronic illnesses, wearable devices have the potential to reduce the frequency of hospitalizations. By allowing users to adjust their daily routines and diets based on device data, these devices can help in timely health management, ultimately leading to cost savings on expensive treatments. Wearable devices can also replace traditional medical equipment like ECG gadgets and defibrillators by providing precise measurements of body temperature and heart health, resulting in potential cost savings for hospitals [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0019)].

Additionally, wearable devices empower patients to take better control of their lives and health. They offer real-time insights into an individual's current physical condition, which can be invaluable in ensuring timely hospitalization. As people increasingly prioritize their health with improving living standards, there has been a surge of interest in heart rate [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0020)], blood oxygen monitoring [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0021),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0022)], sports and fitness, and related equipment [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0023)].

In recent years, the landscape of wearable technology has undergone a significant transformation. Researchers have shifted their focus from tracking regular exercise routines to addressing pressing healthcare challenges, such as diabetic patient management and geriatric remote monitoring [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016)]. To tackle these complex issues, scientists have concentrated on the development of wearable biosensors, which incorporate biometric elements into sensor operations, including enzymes, antibodies, cell receptors, or organelles [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Various biological fluids found in the human body, including tears, sweat, interstitial fluid (ISF), epidermal fluids, and breath, serve as valuable sources for developing devices that enable early disease detection [[[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0024), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0025), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0028)]. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#tbl0001) compiles various biological fluid used for the development of wearable biosensors along with their advantages and limitations.

Table 1. Comparison of Various Biological Fluids for Wearable Biosensing Applications.

| Biological Fluid | Key Source | Accessibility | Biomarker Accuracy | Analytical Complexity | Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Tear** | Lachrymal Glands | Moderate (via eye-wearable devices) | High – due to direct diffusion of analytes from blood | Low – streamlined detection of proteins, electrolytes, and metabolites | High precision and specificity, similar composition to blood | Limited volume, collection can be challenging without irritation |
| **Sweat** | Eccrine and apocrine sweat glands (via skin) | High – readily available across the body surface | Moderate – concentrations can vary based on activity and hydration | Moderate – influenced by sweat rate, evaporation, and contamination | Non-invasive, widely accessible, suitable for continuous monitoring | Biomarker concentration variability, potential environmental interferences |
| **ISF** | Between skin cells and capillaries | Moderate – accessed via microneedles or skin-permeable patches | High – closely reflects blood analyte levels | Moderate to High – requires minimally invasive access tools | High biomarker relevance; stable composition | Requires specialized access methods; slower sample replenishment |
| **Epidermal** | Outer skin layers | Low – small quantities | Low to Moderate | High – complex matrix and low volume | Potential for superficial, non-invasive sampling | Low fluid volume; difficult to isolate specific analytes |
| **Breath** | Lungs (exhaled air) | High – easy collection | Low to Moderate – depends on target gases (e.g., VOCs) | High – complex and variable due to environmental and physiological factors | Non-invasive; continuous sampling possible | Variability due to diet, environment, and metabolism |

Among the various types of wearable biosensors, skin-worn biosensor devices have garnered significant attention, primarily because the epidermis covers a substantial portion of the human body [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0029)]. These devices rely on collecting perspiration from the skin's surface or sampling interstitial fluid, facilitating the flow of biofluids across the biosensor surface. The widespread distribution of sweat glands across the body makes perspiration the most readily accessible biofluid for chemical sensing applications. As a result, these biosensor devices offer a promising avenue for efficient and non-invasive disease monitoring [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0030),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0031)]. These sensors can be attached to the body through various means, including standard clothing, watches, mobile phones, and other everyday items integrated with sensors. Flexible sensors have emerged as a promising innovation, with advances in printed electronics and materials enabling tiny sensors to be used as skin patches. These biosensors are capable of measuring skin conductance, heart rate, body temperature, and detecting changes in pH [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0032),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0033)], glucose [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0034)], and salt levels [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0035),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0036)] within the human body. Abstract
--------

The human body is an intelligent system, continuously generating signals that correlate with specific vital activities and indicate the state of our health and fitness. Therefore, accurate and real-time tracking of these signals is important for monitoring our health and timely medical interventions. The quantification of these signals in real-time is made possible by using skin wearable devices that detect disease-related biomarkers in bodily fluids, such as sweat and interstitial fluid. Integrating nanomaterials, particularly graphene, into wearable devices has dramatically enhanced the performance of wearable biosensors. The exemplary electrical properties, mechanical flexibility, and biocompatibility of graphene have made it a revolutionary material to shape the future of wearable devices. Graphene is versatile because its surface chemistry can be easily tuned to accommodate different biorecognition elements. This review provides an overview of flexible wearable biosensing devices, their sampling methods, and how microfluidic approaches enhance their performance. The paper also discusses the different strategies for the synthesis of graphene nanostructures, their integration into wearable systems, and their ability to improve sensing performance. Various surface chemistry modification techniques are also explored for the enhancement of the immobilisation of biomolecules. Finally, the paper discusses the challenges of graphene-based wearable technologies and their roles in continuous health monitoring and personalised medicine.

Introduction
------------

Wearable biosensors allow users to monitor their health conditions. This technology enables continuous health monitoring, improving disease management, prevention, and timely medical intervention.[1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit1 "Select to navigate to references") A wearable sensing system is an integrated analytical tool that combines point-of-care (PoC) features with wireless connectivity, operating independently as an autonomous system. These devices continuously monitor an individual's physiological and biochemical data non-invasively or with minimal invasiveness. This approach enables the surveillance of subtle physiological fluctuations from baseline values throughout an extended period of time.[2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") Wearable technology has existed for decades. The Holter monitor, for instance, has measured heart activity since the 1960s and has evolved significantly.[3](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit3 "Select to navigate to references") Although wearable devices differ in their applications, they typically consist of the following standard components: substrates, electrode materials, sensors encompassing interfacing, biofluid sampling, analyte biorecognition, generation of signals and their transmission to the amplification components, followed by signal-analysis units that collect, process, and transmit data, as well as the power unit.[4](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit4 "Select to navigate to references")
Today's wearable technology can deliver measurements of a quality level comparable to that obtained from standard medical instruments. Hence, it is becoming difficult to differentiate between wearables targeting consumers and those certified as medical devices. Early wearables focused primarily on biophysical monitoring by measuring metrics such as body temperature, heart rate, physical activity, and weight. However, as these devices evolved, they covered other applications as well.[2,5](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit2 "Select to navigate to reference") Owing to the wide adoption and success of early wearables, the emphasis has increasingly shifted to more efficient, continuous, non-invasive or minimally invasive monitoring approaches, representing a significant stride towards personalised healthcare solutions.[6,7](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit6 "Select to navigate to reference") Second-generation wearable devices include on-skin tattoos, patches, tooth-mounted films, smart textiles, contact lenses, and more invasive options like microneedle-based and injectable devices.[8–10](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit8 "Select to navigate to reference")

Several factors must be thoroughly considered when designing and fabricating a functional wearable device. Consistent and reliable performance is crucial for high-quality devices. In addition, cost-effectiveness and biocompatibility with skin and body fluids are vital if a sensor is considered commercially viable.[11](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit11 "Select to navigate to references") The choice and preparation of materials are instrumental in creating high-quality flexible sensors. This includes the selection of appropriate flexible substrates and conductive filler materials. Material selection and preparation are critical to manufacturing flexible sensors. These materials must retain high conductivity, flexibility, and durability that can hardly be offered by the conventional sensing electrodes used for the past decades.[12](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit12 "Select to navigate to references") The exceptional mechanical properties of such materials are essential for fabricating wearable devices, providing excellent device stability with conformational changes.[13](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit13 "Select to navigate to references")

Therefore, nanomaterials have emerged as promising materials for sensing electrodes, taking over the conventional bulk electrode materials in flexible biosensors. The advances in medical sensor technology heavily depend on advances in nanomaterials.[14,15](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit14 "Select to navigate to reference") Nanostructured materials, including carbon-based nanomaterials, conductive polymers, and metal nanoparticles, have been extensively studied for on-skin wearables owing to their biocompatibility and superior electrical, physical, and chemical characteristics, including their high carrier mobility rate, high thermal conductivity, extraordinary mechanical resilience, and expansive specific surface area.[16–19](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit16 "Select to navigate to reference") These unique characteristics have been exploited in a wide range of applications, including sensors, optics, biology, medicine, and energy storage.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly graphene, enhance biosensor performance due to their exceptional properties.[20](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit20 "Select to navigate to references") Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a perfect honeycomb-like hexagonal lattice. This carbon allotrope is one of the most revolutionary materials of the past two decades, owing to its extraordinary electrical, mechanical, thermal, and optical properties.[21](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit21 "Select to navigate to references") Therefore, graphene has been extensively associated with various applications, such as electronics, optoelectronics, telecommunications, energy storage, healthcare, and biomedical applications.[22](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit22 "Select to navigate to references")

Graphene nanostructures possess unique characteristics for biomedical applications, including superior conductivity, electrochemical stability, large surface area, biocompatibility, mechanical durability, transparency, and cost-effectiveness.[23](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit23 "Select to navigate to references") These features make graphene a cornerstone material for a wide range of medical devices, including optical,[24](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit24 "Select to navigate to references") electrochemical,[25](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit25 "Select to navigate to references") and field-effect transistor (FET) biosensors,[26](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit26 "Select to navigate to references") artificial muscle and skin,[27,28](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit27 "Select to navigate to reference") wearable human–machine interfaces,[29,30](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit29 "Select to navigate to reference") and artificial throats.[31](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit31 "Select to navigate to references")

In recent years, graphene has garnered significant attention in the fields of digital health and portable bioanalytical wearable biosensors due to the unique combination of its properties.[32](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit32 "Select to navigate to references") Graphene has been recognised as an efficient material for wearable sensors because of its light weight, flexibility, high sensitivity, and capability of seamless integration with the human body, accommodating the body's conformational changes, while enabling precise monitoring of parameters of interest in real-time.[33](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit33 "Select to navigate to references") The detection of the relevant parameters is facilitated by graphene's extended surface, excellent electron mobility, and ability to detect minute changes in its environment, making it an ideal candidate for developing next-generation wearable sensing technologies.[34](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit34 "Select to navigate to references")

In this paper, we first provide an overview of the latest advances in wearable technology to highlight the need for functional materials in signal enhancement. This is followed by an introduction to graphene nanostructures as functional materials and their transformative role in advancing wearable systems in healthcare monitoring. This review explores different types of wearable devices, accessible biofluids, and their sampling techniques, as well as relevant signal transduction, enhancement, and analysis essential for designing and deploying wearable devices. Next, we discuss the properties of graphene nanostructures, the effective modification strategies of surface chemistry required for the conjugation of biomolecules, and various signal transduction mechanisms employed in graphene-based wearables ([Fig. 1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure")).

[![Image 11: image file: d5nh00141b-f1.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f1.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 1**Overview of graphene-based wearable biosensors and their application in healthcare, illustrating key properties of graphene, surface chemistry modification for suitable immobilisation of biomolecules, biofluid sampling strategies, and signal transduction mechanisms.

While graphene-based wearable biosensors have been widely reviewed, most existing literature provides broad overviews without a focused analysis on strategies that enable truly seamless and fully wearable applications. Additionally, the role of surface chemistry modifications in ensuring effective immobilisation of biomolecules has not been explored in depth. In this review, we focus on addressing these gaps by exploring graphene and its potential towards novel research strategies, enabling fully wearable applications, alongside an in-depth discussion on the modification of surface chemistry for stable and effective biomolecule immobilisation. In addition, we examine the fundamental elements of wearable sensors to identify current trends and challenges, and recommend the future trajectory of this field.

Wearable devices in healthcare management
-----------------------------------------

Wearable sensors are emerging as a key solution to healthcare monitoring.[35](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") These in-home and point-of-care analytical devices are used to continuously monitor physiological and biological markers through non-invasive or minimally invasive methods. Compared to traditional point-of-care devices, which mostly rely on blood samples, wearable sensors can utilise easily accessible biofluids such as saliva, tears, and sweat to detect clinically significant biomarkers.[36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit36 "Select to navigate to references") The ultimate goal of these devices is the continuous or regular monitoring process of clinically relevant parameters, allowing prompt intervention and preventative measures to be taken. Besides being designed with optimised mobility and accessibility, they should be compact, light, and autonomous, ensuring minimal impact on users' daily lives. A wearable device can be customised to deliver personalised healthcare tailored to each person, especially senior citizens and neonates.[37](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit37 "Select to navigate to references") Machine learning algorithms can be trained using the vast amounts of data they generate, which can be used in artificial intelligence to assist decision-making.[38](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit38 "Select to navigate to references") Despite being expensive at present, these devices are expected to become more affordable in the future.
Wearables of the second generation have a prominent feature of using biofluids via biorecognition elements that transform the presence and concentration of particular analytes into measurable and quantifiable signals.[2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") While most of these products are still in the prototype stage, a few notable commercial exceptions are illustrated in [Fig. 2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure"). Among the most popular wearables in the market for continuous glucose monitoring is the FreeStyle Libre ([Fig. 2a](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")),[39](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit39 "Select to navigate to references") and the Dexcom G7 ([Fig. 2b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")),[40](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit40 "Select to navigate to references") in addition to the Gx Sweat Patch for real-time hydration and electrolyte analysis ([Fig. 2c](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")).[41,42](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit41 "Select to navigate to reference") In recent years, biophysical and biochemical wearable devices have been shown to be valuable tools for detecting and managing diseases and promoting wellness.[43](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit43 "Select to navigate to references") Moreover, wearable device applications extend beyond human-centric health and well-being, with their usage proliferating in monitoring the health of animals for both domestic pet care and animal livestock management purposes.[44](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit44 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 12: image file: d5nh00141b-f2.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f2.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 2**Examples of commercial wearable biosensors for continuous health monitoring. (a) FreeStyle Libre by Abbott for real-time blood glucose monitoring.[39](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit39 "Select to navigate to references") (b) Dexcom G7 by Dexcom for advanced continuous glucose tracking.[40](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit40 "Select to navigate to references") (c) Gx Sweat Patch by Epicore Biosystems (Gatorade) for hydration and electrolyte analysis through sweat.[41,42](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit41 "Select to navigate to reference")

State-of-the-art wearable sensors can provide reliable continuous monitoring of various vitals, including temperature, humidity, blood oxygen levels, blood pressure, electrogastrogram (EEG), and electrocardiogram (ECG). In addition, wearable sensors can access biofluids for monitoring and detecting clinically relevant biomarkers. The primary elements of wearable sensors include the sensing element and signal conditioning circuits. Analog-to-digital converters transform the analog signals into the digital format, while microprocessors or microcontrollers process the output into readable data. The acquired data are then stored in special memory units. Wireless communication modules then transmit the information. The device is usually equipped with a power source to supply the necessary energy for operation and interfaces.[36,45](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit36 "Select to navigate to reference") Collectively, these elements enable monitoring and analysis of various physiological and environmental data, supporting applications in personalised healthcare and wellbeing.

The main goal of wearables is offering convenient and non-invasive means to track various biomarkers at home. To achieve their full potential, wearable devices should integrate all essential components mentioned above within a compact and functional system while enabling regenerated biomarker detection. Despite the significant advances in fabrication and testing methods of sensing platforms, the need for external analysers often conflicts with the core concept of integrated wearable systems. In this sense, much research must be oriented towards associating the sensing platforms with the required electronics and analysers in a compact and miniaturised form.[46–51](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit46 "Select to navigate to reference")

Additionally, device regeneration is a critical feature that enables continuous operation of a wearable sensor. The regeneration efficiency depends directly on the biorecognition element immobilised on the surface, which must remain stable and active under non-physiological conditions to allow continuous biorecognition events to occur.[52](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit52 "Select to navigate to references") Various approaches have been introduced to enable a reversible reaction between the bioreceptor and the target analyte. These strategies, as illustrated in [Fig. 3a–d](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#imgfig3 "Select to navigate to figure"), include: (i) engineered enhancements of the sensor surface, (ii) bond cleavage by chemicals, heat, pH shifts, or light, (iii) electrostatic repulsion, and (iv) magnetic manipulation.[53](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit53 "Select to navigate to references") For example, Gao's group presented a reusable wearable sensor to measure nutrients and metabolites continuously. An electric potential was applied for a short period (under 30 seconds) to achieve regeneration. During this restoration process, the electrode's surface is manipulated, releasing the bound analyte and restoring the sensor to its initial functional state.[54](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit54 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 13: image file: d5nh00141b-f3.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f3.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2025/NH/d5nh00141b/d5nh00141b-f3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 3**Strategies for regenerable sensing. (a) Regeneration by electrode cleaning, either electrically or chemically, followed by buffer coating and immobilisation. (b) Regeneration by cleaving the biochemical bond with light, heat, or chemicals (left) and/or pH modulation (right). (c) Regeneration by electric fields, enabling electrostatic repulsion between the binder and the target molecule. (d) Regeneration by magnetic fields through a facile process of reversible removal and attachment of functional materials to the electrode. Reprinted with permission from ref. [53](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit53 "Select to navigate to references"). Copyright © 2024, Royal Society of Chemistry.

A sensing platform is designed based on biorecognition events between the target analyte and the biorecognition element. In the biofluid, the target analyte interacts with the capturing element, which triggers changes in the device signal. This process enables qualitative and quantitative detection of the target molecule. In a standard biosensing surface, an electrode is immobilised with a biomolecule, which could be an antibody, aptamer, enzyme, peptide, or molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs).[55,56](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit55 "Select to navigate to reference") Choosing the correct bio-recognition elements in wearable sensors is challenging because they must withstand extreme conditions. Antibodies and enzymes are not well suited because they are easily degraded outside their physiological context.[57](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit57 "Select to navigate to references") Currently, MIPs and aptamers are commonly used because they are highly resilient under non-physiological conditions and can be easily regenerated.[53,58](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit53 "Select to navigate to reference") Since wearable devices are designed to track different biomarkers continuously, the regeneration capacity and biocompatibility of these recognition elements are of utmost importance.

For sensor fabrication, a substrate is initially functionalised with a bio-recognition element. Given that most wearable devices rely on electrochemical transduction for signal readout, the underlying substrate is usually an electrode.[59](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit59 "Select to navigate to references") Modifying the substrate successfully is crucial for the design of biosensors. Accordingly, the ideal electrodes must allow efficient and straightforward chemistry with the biomolecule, biocompatibility, resistance to fouling, and exhibit superior electron mobility for efficient signal transduction.[60](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2025/nh/d5nh00141b#cit60 "Select to navigate to references") Following the modification of surface chemistry, the high affinity of the electrode towards the selected biomolecule facilitates the proper bio-immobilisation on the electrode surface.
